title: The rules of the elevator
summary: A new Australian tenant in a Paris building meets the caretaker and asks about the old elevator. He gets a complete set of rules — and one strange one at the end.

body.speaker: Tom
body.text: Madame Rousseau, hello. I am Tom. I live on the fifth floor now.

body.speaker: Madame Rousseau
body.text: I know. The Australian, on the fifth.

body.speaker: Tom
body.text: Yes. I have a question. The elevator. Does it work?

body.speaker: Madame Rousseau
body.text: It works. But you must know the rules.

body.speaker: Tom
body.text: There are rules for the elevator?

body.speaker: Madame Rousseau
body.text: There are rules for everything in this building. The elevator is old — 1958. It still works. We are proud.

body.speaker: Tom
body.text: Okay. Tell me.

body.speaker: Madame Rousseau
body.text: Rule one. Only two people. Not three. Three is too heavy.

body.speaker: Tom
body.text: Two people. Got it.

body.speaker: Madame Rousseau
body.text: Rule two. Always close the outside door. Or the elevator will not come.

body.speaker: Tom
body.text: Outside door. Yes.

body.speaker: Madame Rousseau
body.text: Rule three. Never run inside it.

body.speaker: Tom
body.text: I do not plan to run inside an elevator.

body.speaker: Madame Rousseau
body.text: Children plan. Adults forget. Last year a man on the fourth kicked the door. The elevator did not move for one week. Eight hundred euros.

body.speaker: Tom
body.text: I understand.

body.speaker: Madame Rousseau
body.text: Last rule. If you are between floors and the lights go off, do not panic. Sing.

body.speaker: Tom
body.text: Sing?

body.speaker: Madame Rousseau
body.text: Yes. It helps the person inside, while I push the button down here. The voice helps me find which floor you are on.

body.speaker: Tom
body.text: Madame Rousseau, you are a good neighbour.

body.speaker: Madame Rousseau
body.text: I am a good caretaker. Neighbours are next door. I am downstairs. It is different.

annotations.meaningVi: người trông coi toà nhà / bảo vệ chung cư

grammarNotes.title: Modal 'must' and imperatives for rules
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Khi đưa ra quy định, A2 dùng 'must + động từ nguyên thể' (bắt buộc) hoặc câu mệnh lệnh đơn. 'You must know the rules.' 'Always close the outside door.' 'Never run inside it.' 'Do not panic.' Phủ định mạnh là 'must not' hoặc 'never + động từ'. Lưu ý 'must' không thêm '-s' ở ngôi he/she/it: 'He must close the door.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: For rules, A2 uses 'must + base verb' or direct imperatives. 'You must know the rules.' 'Always close the outside door.' 'Never run inside it.' 'Do not panic.' 'Must' never takes an -s: 'He must close the door.' For strong prohibitions, use 'must not' or 'never + verb'.

translationVi:
Tom: Chào bà Rousseau. Tôi là Tom. Giờ tôi sống ở tầng năm.
Bà Rousseau: Tôi biết. Người Úc, tầng năm.
Tom: Vâng. Tôi có một câu hỏi. Cái thang máy. Nó có chạy không ạ?
Bà Rousseau: Có chạy. Nhưng cậu phải biết quy định.
Tom: Có cả quy định cho thang máy ạ?
Bà Rousseau: Toà nhà này thứ gì cũng có quy định. Thang máy này cũ — từ năm 1958. Vẫn chạy. Chúng tôi tự hào.
Tom: Được rồi. Bà nói đi ạ.
Bà Rousseau: Quy định một. Chỉ hai người. Không ba. Ba người là nặng.
Tom: Hai người. Hiểu rồi.
Bà Rousseau: Quy định hai. Luôn đóng cửa ngoài. Không thì thang máy không lên.
Tom: Cửa ngoài. Vâng.
Bà Rousseau: Quy định ba. Không bao giờ chạy bên trong thang máy.
Tom: Tôi đâu có ý định chạy trong thang máy.
Bà Rousseau: Trẻ con thì có ý định. Người lớn thì hay quên. Năm ngoái có một anh tầng tư bực mình, đá vào cửa. Một tuần thang máy không chạy. Tám trăm euro.
Tom: Tôi hiểu rồi.
Bà Rousseau: Quy định cuối. Nếu cậu kẹt giữa hai tầng và đèn tắt, đừng hoảng. Hát.
Tom: Hát ạ?
Bà Rousseau: Ừ. Vừa giúp người bên trong, vừa giúp tôi dưới này biết cậu ở tầng nào để bấm nút.
Tom: Bà Rousseau, bà là một người hàng xóm tốt.
Bà Rousseau: Tôi là một người bảo vệ tốt. Hàng xóm là người sát vách. Tôi ở tầng dưới. Khác đấy.

questions.explanation: 'I live on the fifth floor now.'
Madame Rousseau calls him 'The Australian, on the fifth.'
questions.hint: Madame Rousseau gives him a nationality.

questions.prompt: How many people can use the elevator at one time?
questions.explanation: 'Rule one. Only two people. Not three. Three is too heavy.'

questions.prompt: Why must you close the outside door?
options: Or the elevator will not come.
Or the cleaner will be angry.
questions.explanation: 'Always close the outside door. Or the elevator will not come.'

questions.prompt: What happened last year on the fourth floor?
options: A man kicked the door, and the elevator did not move for one week — eight hundred euros.
questions.explanation: Madame Rousseau tells the story to explain rule three.
questions.hint: Look at her example after rule three.

options: If you are between floors and the lights go off, do not panic — sing.
questions.explanation: 'If you are between floors and the lights go off, do not panic. Sing.'

questions.prompt: Why does Madame Rousseau ask people to sing?
options: It calms the person inside, and her voice lets her know which floor to push the button for.
The elevator has a sound sensor.
questions.explanation: 'It helps the person inside, while I push the button down here. The voice helps me find which floor you are on.'
questions.hint: Madame Rousseau explains the singing rule.

questions.prompt: How does Madame Rousseau describe herself when Tom calls her 'a good neighbour'?
options: She corrects him: she is a caretaker, not a neighbour — neighbours are next door, she is downstairs.
She says she is not friendly.
She says she is the owner of the building.
questions.explanation: 'I am a good caretaker. Neighbours are next door. I am downstairs. It is different.'

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: Old shared things in a building have informal rules — and one person who quietly knows them all.
Old elevators are dangerous and should be replaced.
Foreigners should not move into French buildings.
Singing is a French national habit.
questions.explanation: The elevator is held together by knowledge — much of it stored in Madame Rousseau.
questions.hint: Think about what keeps the elevator working — beyond the rules themselves.

Tom: There are rules for the elevator?
Madame Rousseau: There are rules for everything in this building. The elevator is old — {{b4}}. It still works. We are proud.
Tom: Okay. Tell me.
Madame Rousseau: Rule one. Only {{b5}} people. Not three. Three is too heavy.
Tom: Two people. Got it.
Madame Rousseau: Rule two. Always close the {{b6}} door. Or the elevator will not come.
Tom: Outside door. Yes.

Tom: I do not plan to run inside an elevator.
Madame Rousseau: Children plan. Adults {{b8}}. Last year a man on the fourth kicked the door. The elevator did not move for one week. Eight hundred euros.
Tom: I understand.
Madame Rousseau: Last rule. If you are between floors and the lights go off, do not {{b9}}. Sing.
Tom: Sing?
Madame Rousseau: Yes. It helps the person inside, while I push the button down here. The voice helps me find which floor you are on.

blanks.explanation: He lives on the fifth floor.
Only two people at a time.
The outside door must be closed.
She is downstairs, not next door.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Madame Rousseau insists she is a caretaker, not a neighbour. Why might she draw that line? Is there a role in your own life — colleague, teacher, sibling — that you would not want anyone to mix up with another word?
